name: IMDb / Letterboxd / MovieLens Folksonomy (user-contributed film tags)
core_claim: The central claim is implicit and bottom-up rather than theoretical: that the words real viewers spontaneously reach for when tagging and reviewing films constitute the most authentic available vocabulary for what a film is and how it lands. Folksonomy (Vander Wal, 2005) — collaborative/social tagging on IMDb plot-keywords, MovieLens tags, and Letterboxd review-tags — assumes no a-priori model of why people enjoy stories; instead it lets a large population emit free-text labels and treats the emergent distribution as signal. The key claim relevant to HelpME2C is that this emergent vocabulary is HETEROGENEOUS by design: a single corpus surfaces genre ('sci-fi'), plot structure ('flashback', 'plot-twist', 'twist-ending'), production/metadata ('based-on-a-book', '1970s'), AND — crucially — affective/experiential descriptors ('feel-good', 'thought-provoking', 'depressing', 'boring', 'cute', 'suspenseful', 'dark', 'comfort-movie', 'made-me-cry'). The folksonomy's implicit thesis is that no single dimension drives enjoyment; viewers describe films along whatever axis is salient to them, and the recommender's job is to mine that mixed bag. The academic distillation of this is the MPST corpus (Kar et al., 2018), which collapsed ~134k+157k raw IMDb/MovieLens tags down to 71 fine-grained 'storyline-related' tags by frequency-filtering (kept only tags assigned to >=100 movies) and clustering semantically similar tags (e.g. cult/cult-film/cult-movie -> 'cult'; suspenseful/suspense/tense -> 'suspenseful').
descriptive_vs_experiential: Uniquely among the frameworks in this set, the folksonomy is a MIXTURE across all four levels, not a clean occupant of one — and that is its defining characteristic and its danger. Within the same 71-tag MPST tagset you find: DESCRIPTIVE/topical ('murder', 'sci-fi', 'historical', 'paranormal', 'revenge', 'christian-film', 'autobiographical', 'home-movie'); STRUCTURAL ('flashback', 'nonlinear', 'storytelling', 'plot-twist', 'twist-ending' — flashback is among the four most frequent tags in the corpus alongside murder/violence/romantic); AFFECTIVE/EXPERIENTIAL — the felt-experience band the other descriptive taxonomies lack — ('feel-good', 'thought-provoking', 'depressing', 'boring', 'cute', 'dramatic', 'sentimental', 'entertaining', 'inspiring', 'dark', 'suspenseful', 'psychedelic', 'atmospheric', 'haunting', 'absurd'); and VALUES/CONTENT-INTENSITY ('violence', 'cruelty', 'sadist', 'pornographic', 'gore'). On Letterboxd specifically, the free-tag and review language skews MORE experiential and viewer-relational than IMDb's plot-keyword tradition: tags and review phrases like 'comfort movie', 'made me cry', 'cinema', 'vibes', 'peak fiction', 'no thoughts head empty', 'this changed me' are affective/relational utterances about the viewing experience, not descriptions of content. So: IMDb plot-keywords lean descriptive+structural; MovieLens tags are mixed; Letterboxd review-language is the most experiential/affective and the most receiver-dependent of the three.
key_dimensions: There is no fixed axis set — that is the point of a folksonomy — but the MPST distillation gives the best-curated concrete list of 71 emergent tags grouped here by the level they occupy: (1) AFFECTIVE/EXPERIENTIAL (the candidate additions most novel to HelpME2C): feel-good, thought-provoking, depressing, boring, entertaining, dramatic, sentimental, inspiring, cute, dark, suspenseful, atmospheric, psychedelic, haunting, absurd, melodrama, neo-noir, gothic. (2) CONTENT-INTENSITY / VALUES: violence, cruelty, gore, sadist, pornographic, blaxploitation, brainwashing. (3) STRUCTURAL: flashback, nonlinear, storytelling, plot-twist, twist-ending, alternate-reality, alternate-history. (4) DESCRIPTIVE/TOPICAL: murder, revenge, mystery, sci-fi, fantasy, horror, comedy, romantic, action, paranormal, supernatural, historical, autobiographical, christian-film, philosophical, satire, prank, allegory, anti-war, avant-garde, cult, home-movie, insanity, non-fiction, suicidal, claustrophobic, good-versus-evil, queer, humor, tragedy, dramatic, clever, realism, intrigue, comic, magical-realism, western. The Letterboxd-native additions not in MPST: comfort-movie, rewatchable, slow-burn (shared with HelpME2C), 'made-me-cry'/tearjerker, cozy, vibey, style-over-substance, so-bad-its-good, fever-dream, devastating. Each MPST tag is also annotated (Kar et al.) with an EMOTION-FLOW signature via NRC lexicons (joy/trust/anger/disgust/sadness/fear over 20 plot segments) — e.g. cute/feel-good/romantic plots show joy+trust dominance; violent/dark/gothic/suspenseful plots show fear+anger+sadness dominance.
cross_cultural_validity: Strongly Western/English-centric and NOT cross-culturally validated. The raw inputs are IMDb (US-founded, English-dominant contributor base), MovieLens (University of Minnesota research userbase, overwhelmingly US/English), and Letterboxd (NZ-founded, anglophone cinephile community). The MPST corpus is entirely English plot-synopses with English tags, and the emotion-flow analysis uses the NRC Emotion Lexicon which, while translated into many languages, was psychometrically built in English. [uncertain] No published study validates that the emergent tag distribution would look the same in a Mandarin, Hindi, Arabic, or Spanish film-tagging community, and there are strong reasons to expect divergence: affective tags like 'feel-good', 'cozy', 'wholesome', 'cringe', 'vibes' are anglophone-internet idioms that do not translate cleanly; 'christian-film' as a salient category is US-specific; humour and 'absurd'/'camp' calibration is culturally bound; content-intensity sensibilities ('pornographic', sexual-suggestiveness) mirror US/MPAA anxieties. What is most portable: the descriptive-topical and structural tags (murder, flashback, sci-fi, twist-ending) survive translation; the affective and values tags are the least portable. The folksonomy also inherits a POPULARITY/COVERAGE bias that interacts with culture: ~34% of the top 130k IMDb movies have NO tags at all, and tagging density concentrates on Western/popular titles, so the emergent vocabulary over-represents what an English-speaking enthusiast audience watches and under-represents world cinema.
empirical_grounding: Grounded in BEHAVIOURAL/CROWD data of a particular kind — millions of voluntary, unprompted tagging acts — but only weakly validated against ENJOYMENT, completion, or preference. The grounding is best described as ECOLOGICAL (these are the words real people actually used, at scale) plus COMPUTATIONAL (MPST demonstrates the 71 tags can be predicted from plot synopses above baseline, and that tag co-occurrence — measured by Positive Pointwise Mutual Information — recovers sensible structure: 'cute/entertaining/dramatic/sentimental' cluster with 'feel-good' at high PPMI, while 'sadist/cruelty/insanity/violence' show low PPMI with 'feel-good', matching real-world intuition). It is NOT experimental: no controlled study shows that a 'feel-good' tag predicts that a given viewer will enjoy or complete the film. MovieLens tags DO sit inside a ratings ecosystem so tag-rating correlations are studiable, but the tags themselves are noisy and redundant (Katakis et al., 2008 — the original motivation for cleaning). Quality of evidence: strong on FACE VALIDITY and INTER-TAG STRUCTURE (PPMI heatmaps, emotion-flow curves that distinguish happy-ending vs tragic plots), weak on PREDICTIVE VALIDITY against actual enjoyment. To validate LLM-extracted dimensions against this framework: MPST is freely downloadable with train/test splits, so you can directly measure LLM-from-synopsis tag-prediction against human-folksonomy gold labels (tag-recall, micro-F1) — MPST's own logistic-regression baselines (micro-F1 ~37, tag-recall ~17 for top-5) set a low bar an LLM should beat. But beating tag-prediction is NOT the same as validating against enjoyment; the honest test is whether folksonomy-derived affective tags predict your joint-watch / completion signal.
operationalisability: Highly relevant and partially extractable — with a sharp split by tag level. The MPST paper IS an operationalisability experiment: it predicts the 71 tags from plot synopses (avg ~986 words) using lexical n-grams, sentiment/affective-dimension features, semantic frames, and emotion-flow, beating majority/random baselines but with modest absolute scores. Extraction unit: WHOLE WORK (tags are title-level; episodic/arc nuance lost). For an LLM reading a SHORT synopsis (note MPST used long Wikipedia/IMDb synopses, ~986 words, not loglines — performance on a 1-2 sentence synopsis will be materially worse): DESCRIPTIVE+STRUCTURAL tags (murder, sci-fi, flashback, revenge, based-on) are the most reliably extractable. AFFECTIVE tags split: some are inferable as priors from plot content (a synopsis describing a dog dying signals 'depressing'/'tearjerker'; a heist-gone-right with banter signals 'feel-good'/'entertaining'), but the most valuable experiential tags are EXECUTION-DEPENDENT and NOT recoverable from synopsis — 'atmospheric', 'boring', 'slow-burn', 'thought-provoking', 'haunting', 'so-bad-its-good', 'comfort-movie', 'vibey' live in pacing, cinematography, performance, and the viewer's relationship to the film, none of which a plot summary contains. Failure modes for an LLM: (1) marketing/synopsis-copy bias inflating 'entertaining'/'inspiring'; (2) cannot grade intensity ('violence' yes/no, not how-much); (3) twist-ending/conclusiveness is spoiler-protected and absent from synopses; (4) flattening of tone (the same plot tagged 'dark-comedy' by some and 'depressing' by others — the folksonomy itself disagrees, so there is no single gold label); (5) inability to produce receiver-relational tags ('comfort-movie' is a property of a viewer's history, not the film).
motivation_served: Because the vocabulary is emergent, it spans the full motivational range without committing to any — and this is its richest contribution. Tags map onto multiple need-states simultaneously: HEDONIC/fun ('entertaining', 'feel-good', 'fun', 'cute', 'so-bad-its-good'); EUDAIMONIC/meaning ('thought-provoking', 'inspiring', 'devastating', 'haunting', 'this-changed-me'); MOOD-REGULATION ('comfort-movie', 'cozy', 'rewatchable', 'turn-your-brain-off'); IDENTITY/expression (Letterboxd tags as taste-signalling — 'peak cinema', 'cinema', genre-allegiance tags); SOCIAL CONNECTION (review-language performed for an audience of followers). For GROUP viewing this is double-edged: the affective tags are exactly the currency two people negotiate in ('I want something light/feel-good', 'I want something that'll make us think'), so a folksonomy-derived affective vocabulary directly serves the two-person trade-off HelpME2C is built for — far better than a purely descriptive taxonomy. But the receiver-relational tags ('comfort-movie') cannot be shared between two people because comfort is personal; the system must hold the affective tag as a per-viewer PREFERENCE and the descriptive tags as shared CONSTRAINTS.
receiver_dependence: The folksonomy contains a clean gradient from absolute to relational, and sorting tags along it is the single most useful exercise for HelpME2C. ABSOLUTE (property of the work): descriptive/structural/intensity tags — 'murder', 'flashback', 'sci-fi', 'violence', 'twist-ending', '1970s', 'based-on-a-book' are viewer-independent facts about the film. PARTIALLY RELATIONAL (work property but with viewer-dependent calibration): mid-affective tags — 'dark', 'suspenseful', 'atmospheric', 'feel-good', 'depressing' are mostly properties of the work but where the threshold is viewer-set (what one viewer calls 'feel-good' another calls 'saccharine'; what one calls 'slow-burn' another calls 'boring' — note 'boring' and 'slow-burn' can tag the same film). FULLY RELATIONAL (emerges only from work x specific viewer): 'comfort-movie', 'rewatchable', 'made-me-cry', 'this-changed-me', 'overrated', 'so-bad-its-good' are statements about a viewer's relationship to the film and CANNOT be tagged on the work alone. This gradient is a warning: a naive system that ingests folksonomy tags as if they were all work-properties will mistake relational tags ('comfort-movie') for absolute ones and produce nonsense. HelpME2C should explicitly partition: ingest absolute tags as work facts, model partially-relational tags as work-attribute-plus-per-viewer-threshold, and treat fully-relational tags as viewer-side preference signals NOT work tags.
blind_spots: (1) NOISE, SYNONYMY, REDUNDANCY — the defining flaw the entire MPST cleaning pipeline exists to fix: raw tags include spam, idiosyncratic single-user tags ('panty color'-type irrelevancies cited in IMDb keyword critiques), and dozens of synonyms for one concept; uncleaned folksonomy is unusable as a controlled vocabulary. (2) COVERAGE / LONG-TAIL SPARSITY — ~34% of top IMDb movies have zero tags; tagging concentrates on popular, recent, Western, fan-beloved titles, so the vocabulary and the data are both biased toward what enthusiasts watch. (3) NO CONTROLLED STRUCTURE — flat tag-space, no hierarchy, no orthogonality; the same film accrues contradictory tags ('underrated' AND 'overrated'). (4) RELATIONAL/ABSOLUTE CONFUSION (above) — the folksonomy mixes work-facts and viewer-feelings in one undifferentiated bag. (5) NO INTENSITY GRADING — tags are binary presence/absence ('violence' is there or not; not 1-5). (6) POPULARITY-DRIVEN — frequent tags (murder/violence/flashback/romantic) reflect what gets tagged, not what matters for matching. (7) TEMPORAL/SLANG DRIFT — Letterboxd affective idiom ('vibes', 'peak fiction', 'cinema', 'no thoughts head empty') is fast-moving and cohort-specific, ageing badly. (8) PERFORMATIVE BIAS — Letterboxd reviews are written to be read by followers (jokes, references, taste-signalling), so the language is theatrical, not a clean read on felt experience. (9) GROUP-BLIND — no native two-person machinery. (10) optimises (where it optimises at all) for what's MEMORABLE/SHAREABLE about a film, not what predicts joint enjoyment — off HelpME2C's North Star.
vocabulary_implications: The folksonomy is the single best EMPIRICAL SOURCE for which affective theme-slugs are worth having, because it shows what words real viewers actually emit — but it must be mined, cleaned, and partitioned, never ingested raw. Concrete proposals: (1) ADD a small set of AFFECTIVE/TONE slugs that the folksonomy proves are high-frequency and that HelpME2C's largely topical/structural existing list lacks: 'feel-good' (vs the existing dark-comedy/absurdist-comedy which are humour-types not mood), 'thought-provoking' (overlaps existing meditation-on-* and character-study but is a distinct felt-quality), 'depressing'/'bleak', 'comfort-watch' (FLAG: receiver-relational — see below), 'atmospheric', 'suspenseful' (partially covered by existing slow-burn/mystery-investigation but suspense is a felt axis, not a structure). (2) VALIDATE existing slugs against folksonomy frequency: slow-burn is corroborated (Letterboxd-native and high-salience); the speculative cluster (cosmic-horror, folk-horror, post-apocalyptic-survival) maps onto folksonomy descriptive tags and is safe; meditation-on-death/meditation-on-time map onto 'philosophical'/'thought-provoking'. (3) DO NOT add fully-relational tags as WORK tags: 'comfort-movie', 'rewatchable', 'made-me-cry', 'overrated', 'so-bad-its-good' must be modelled as PER-VIEWER preference/relationship signals, NOT controlled-vocabulary slugs — flag explicitly. (4) NOT EXTRACTABLE FROM SYNOPSIS ALONE (require other data — reviews, transcripts, pacing metadata, or a human/heuristic pass): 'atmospheric', 'boring'/'slow-burn-vs-boring', 'thought-provoking', 'haunting', intensity of 'violence', twist-ending presence, and all receiver-relational tags. Treat any LLM-from-synopsis affective value as a LOW-CONFIDENCE prior; the high-confidence extractables are descriptive+structural. (5) METHOD: use the folksonomy (MPST 71 tags + Letterboxd review mining) as a DISCOVERY corpus to propose candidate slugs and to sanity-check that proposed slugs are words humans actually use, then cluster synonyms (MPST-style) before adopting; do not adopt the flat 71-tag list wholesale.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: The folksonomy is the most VALIDATING of all frameworks in the set because HelpME2C's slug list is itself a curated folksonomy-like vocabulary, and the emergent tags confirm large parts of it while exposing a systematic gap. VALIDATES: the Texture cluster directly (slow-burn, nonlinear-storytelling, magical-realism, absurdist-comedy, dark-comedy, satire, character-study, meditation-on-* all appear as or map onto real folksonomy tags — flashback/nonlinear/storytelling/satire/philosophical/magical-realism are all in MPST's 71); the Speculative cluster (cosmic-horror, folk-horror, existential-horror, post-apocalyptic-survival, dystopian-society) maps onto folksonomy horror/sci-fi/paranormal/apocalyptic descriptive tags; the genre/structure cluster (mystery-investigation, whodunit, heist-and-caper) maps onto murder/mystery/intrigue tags. REVEALS GAPS: (a) HelpME2C has almost NO pure-MOOD/affective slugs — the folksonomy's most frequent and most decision-relevant tags ('feel-good', 'thought-provoking', 'depressing', 'suspenseful', 'entertaining', 'atmospheric', 'cute') have no clean home in a list that is topical/structural/relational-theme-based; this is the biggest actionable gap. (b) No content-INTENSITY grading (consistent with the Netflix finding) — folksonomy has 'violence'/'cruelty'/'gore' as presence tags, HelpME2C has war-and-its-toll/vengeance-and-its-cost as themes but no intensity facet. (c) No receiver-relational layer ('comfort-watch', 'rewatchable') — the existing list assumes work-side description suffices. SUGGESTS MISALIGNMENT: none of the existing slugs is contradicted, but the folksonomy warns that HelpME2C's list, like any curated vocabulary, risks over-representing 'serious'/literary themes (grief-and-loss, complicity-with-evil, legacy-of-empire) while under-representing the light/hedonic/mood vocabulary that real viewers use most when choosing what to watch together tonight — exactly the two-person decision HelpME2C serves. Net: add a dedicated MOOD/affect facet (feel-good, thought-provoking, depressing, suspenseful, atmospheric, cute/wholesome), add an intensity facet, and add a per-viewer relational layer for comfort/rewatch signals; keep the topical/structural slugs.
key_sources: PRIMARY (free): Sudipta Kar, Suraj Maharjan, A. Pastor Lopez-Monroy, Thamar Solorio, 'MPST: A Corpus of Movie Plot Synopses with Tags,' LREC 2018, arXiv:1802.07858 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.07858) — the canonical academic distillation of IMDb+MovieLens folksonomy into 71 cleaned fine-grained tags over 14,828 movies, with PPMI tag-correlation and NRC emotion-flow analysis; corpus freely downloadable at http://ritual.uh.edu/mpst-2018/. RELATED (free): Sudipta Kar et al., 'Folksonomication: Predicting Tags for Movies from Plot Synopses Using Emotion Flow Encoded Neural Network,' COLING 2018, arXiv:1808.04943 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1808.04943). Thomas Vander Wal, coinage of 'folksonomy' (2005, vanderwal.net/folksonomy.html). MovieLens tagging noise: Katakis et al. 2008; F. Maxwell Harper & Joseph Konstan, 'The MovieLens Datasets,' ACM TiiS 2016 (https://doi.org/10.1145/2827872). FOLKSONOMY/CINEMA EVOLUTION (free): 'Quantitative analysis of the evolution of novelty in cinema through crowdsourced keywords,' arXiv:1304.0786 (uses IMDb crowdsourced keywords). LETTERBOXD (free, journalistic/community): Letterboxd Journal, 'Tag Team: how to optimize your Letterboxd activity with the simple art of the tag' (https://letterboxd.com/journal/tag-team-how-to-use-letterboxd-tags/) [403 on direct fetch — accessible via browser]; INFO 653 'Folksonomy on Letterboxd' (https://lis653.wordpress.com/2026/02/18/folksonomy-on-letterboxd/); Letterboxd help, 'How do tags work?' (https://letterboxd.zendesk.com/hc/en-us/articles/15179274026639). DATASETS (free): pkchwy/letterboxd-all-movie-data on Hugging Face (~847k films incl. reviews) (https://huggingface.co/datasets/pkchwy/letterboxd-all-movie-data); sdl60660/letterboxd_recommendations (GitHub). IMDb keyword quality critique: IMDb Community Forums 'inappropriate plot keywords' thread. NOTE: IMDb's full keyword list and Letterboxd's full tag/review corpus are not officially published as research datasets; MPST is the cleanest freely-usable distillation.
